stem:
A 4-month-old boy is brought to the paediatric emergency department by his parents in winter with 3 days of runny nose, cough and noisy breathing. He has been feeding only half his usual volumes for 24 hours and his nappies are drier. He was born at 38 weeks and has had no previous admissions. His parents are non-smokers and he is up to date with immunisations.

He has been on nasal prong oxygen 1.5 L/min for 2 hours since arrival. The bedside nurse asks you to review him because his work of breathing has worsened over the last 20 minutes.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168, BP 86/52 mmHg, RR 72, SpO2 89% on nasal prong 1.5 L/min, temperature 37.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert but tiring, marked subcostal and intercostal recession, head bobbing, widespread fine inspiratory crackles and expiratory wheeze, capillary refill 2 seconds, no apnoeas observed
data_table.Investigations: Capillary blood gas pH 7.30, pCO2 56 mmHg, HCO3 26, lactate 1.6; nasopharyngeal viral PCR pending; chest X-ray hyperinflation with no focal consolidation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in respiratory support?

options.text: Commence humidified high-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min
options.rationale: RCH bronchiolitis pathway escalates to HFNC at 2 L/kg/min after failure of low-flow nasal prong (defined by persisting work of breathing, SpO2 below 90% despite 1-2 L/min, or rising CO2) for at least 1-2 hours. HFNC provides PEEP and washout of upper-airway dead space; trials including PARIS support its use for moderate-severe bronchiolitis under 12 months. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Start nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg back-to-back for three doses
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months and add no benefit in this age group; trials show no change in admission rate or oxygen requirement. They are not part of the RCH bronchiolitis algorithm. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Give a single intramuscular dose of dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not change outcome in bronchiolitis and are not recommended in the RCH guideline. Dexamethasone has a role in croup, not bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

Intubation is reserved for true respiratory failure (severe acidosis, recurrent apnoeas, exhaustion despite HFNC). A pH of 7.30 with mild hypercapnia and a tiring but alert infant is an indication to escalate to HFNC before considering invasive ventilation. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Trial nebulised hypertonic saline 3% with chest physiotherapy
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline has inconsistent evidence and is not first-line escalation in RCH guidance. Routine chest physiotherapy is contraindicated in bronchiolitis as it does not improve outcomes and can worsen distress. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

explanation.summary: RCH bronchiolitis pathway uses low-flow nasal prong oxygen 1-2 L/min as first-line, with SpO2 target of 90%. HFNC at 2 L/kg/min is the next-step escalation when low-flow fails after a 1-2 hour trial, indicated by persisting work of breathing, falling SpO2, or rising CO2. Bronchodilators, steroids and routine physiotherapy are NOT used in bronchiolitis under 12 months. Intubation is reserved for HFNC failure or apnoeas.
key_points: RCH SpO2 target in bronchiolitis is 90% (not 92-94%)
Low-flow 1-2 L/min first; HFNC 2 L/kg/min only after low-flow fails
Bronchodilators, steroids and routine physiotherapy not recommended under 12 months
explanation.pearls: PARIS trial supports HFNC for escalation only; do not start it before a low-flow trial as it does not reduce ICU admission compared with standard oxygen as primary therapy.
